title: Re-examining Maruyama Masao’s “Origin Myth”: Focusing on the Early Manuscripts Collected in the Tokyo Woman’s Christian University Masao Maruyama Collection
excerpt: As a representative figure of postwar democracy in Japan, Maruyama Masao has long served as a source of intellectual inspiration. This article re-examines the so-called origin myth surrounding Maruyama’s early thought by focusing on early manuscripts preserved in the Tokyo Woman’s Christian University Masao Maruyama Collection.
body: As a representative figure of postwar democracy in Japan, Maruyama Masao has long served as a source of intellectual inspiration, and a relatively fixed image of his thought has taken shape in subsequent scholarship. This article offers a different reading of the “origin myth” surrounding Maruyama, a myth constructed around his detention in 1933. Drawing on early manuscripts that have not yet received sufficient scholarly attention, it reexamines Maruyama’s early thought in its historical context. The article argues that this “myth of origins” is difficult to sustain. Nationalism and elitism constituted two important threads running through Maruyama’s early thought. His early political position remained largely consistent with the existing order of power. In confronting radical currents on both the left and the right, he defended the established system by taking nationalism as a premise and elitism as a means: he defined the qualifications for political participation while seeking to claim the discourse of patriotism. Reconsidering Maruyama’s early image is therefore essential for reinterpreting his later canonical theories, including his critique of the Tennōsei.

title: Japanese Communists and Their Entanglements with the International Communist Movement, 1920s–1950s: The Japanese Communist Party and the New Left in Global Networks
excerpt: This thesis examines the transnational networks that shaped Japanese communism from the Comintern era to the emergence of the Japanese New Left. Focusing on figures such as Nosaka Sanzō and on the JCP’s shifting revolutionary strategies, it traces how Soviet and Chinese influences transformed the party’s politics before and after the Korean War. It argues that the failure of armed struggle and the crisis of Stalinism in the 1950s opened the way for a new generation of left-wing movements to challenge the JCP’s claim to revolutionary leadership.
body:
The Japanese Communist Party was founded under the guidance of the Third International and, from its inception, maintained close ties with left-wing movements abroad. Although the JCP was organizationally dismantled under the severe repression of the Japanese state in the 1930s, communists who remained in Japan, together with party members in exile overseas, did not abandon the revolutionary cause. Many of these exiles, under the leadership of the Third International, sought to build networks connecting information, personnel, and material resources between Japan and overseas bases such as Siberia, China, and the West Coast of the United States. Nosaka Sanzō was a representative figure in this process. After arriving in Moscow in 1931 and entering the work of the Third International, Nosaka’s central task was to establish channels of communication with the surviving communist forces inside Japan, in the service of both Soviet foreign policy and revolutionary and antiwar activities in Japan. His activities on the West Coast of the United States and in Yan’an should therefore be understood as integral parts of this broader effort.

After Japan’s defeat in 1945, Nosaka returned to Japan and soon provided the JCP with a general line of peaceful revolution. His vision of the postwar order and revolution had been formed through his work for the Third International; it was also endorsed by both the Chinese Communist Party and the Communist Party of the Soviet Union, and accorded with postwar Soviet foreign policy and national interests. Yet with the intensification of Cold War confrontation and the outbreak of the Korean War, the CCP and the CPSU turned their attention not only to the Korean battlefield but also to Japan itself. They demanded that the JCP abandon the line of peaceful revolution and initiate armed struggle, thereby supporting the war effort in Korea by weakening Japan’s capacity for military supply and attacking American bases in Japan. These Sino-Soviet demands produced a split within the JCP, but the division was soon resolved through Stalin’s intervention. During the period of armed struggle, Japanese communists and their affiliated organizations relied heavily on the experience of the Chinese Revolution in matters of strategy and tactics, revealing a clear tendency to “take China as their teacher.”

The externally imposed line of armed struggle, however, did not correspond to Japan’s specific historical and political conditions. After the failure of this struggle and the loss of broad popular support, the JCP announced at its Sixth National Conference in 1955 a return to a legal line of parliamentary struggle. The defeat of armed struggle and the subsequent shift in party line produced a profound psychological shock among Japanese communists. At the same time, the anti-Stalinist wave triggered by the Twentieth Congress of the CPSU in 1956 and the Hungarian Revolution further stimulated the Japanese left, prompting many activists to question the JCP’s monopoly over revolutionary leadership. In this process, oppositional forces emerged within student organizations that had previously been under JCP control, while Japanese Trotskyists, who had long remained marginal, also became increasingly active. Ultimately, the founding of the Communist League and the Revolutionary Communist League of Japan marked the birth of the Japanese New Left. The Japanese New Left regarded existing revolutionary parties such as the JCP and the CPSU as Stalinist bureaucratic organizations. Some called for a return to the great traditions of Leninism and the October Revolution, while others sought to revive Trotsky’s pre-October revolutionary thought. In all cases, however, they invoked the classics of communist revolution and reinterpreted revolutionary history in order to contest the JCP’s claim to revolutionary leadership.
